mnemonics.title: Key Types Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: SUPER KEY is the family name: All Keys derive from Super Key. CANDIDATE KEY = Super Key - Redundancy. PRIMARY KEY = Selected Candidate. ALTERNATE = Rejected Candidate. FOREIGN = Links Tables.

mnemonics.title: Remember Integrity Rules
mnemonics.content: ENTITY = E very P rimary K ey must have values (E-P-K). REFERENTIAL = F oreign K eys R eference P rimary K eys (F-K-R-P-K).

mnemonics.title: Degree vs Cardinality
mnemonics.content: DEGREE = Down the columns (vertical count). CARDINALITY = Count around the table (horizontal rows). D for Degree/Down, C for Cardinality/Count.

mnemonics.title: Keys Properties
mnemonics.content: PRIMARY: Unique + Not Null + One only. CANDIDATE: Minimal + Unique + Can be PK. FOREIGN: References + Can be Null + Establishes relationship.

acronyms.expansion: Primary Key - Selected unique identifier for a relation
acronyms.usage: Used to uniquely identify each tuple in a table, cannot contain NULL values

acronyms.expansion: Foreign Key - Attribute referencing another relation's PK
acronyms.usage: Establishes relationships between tables, enforces referential integrity

acronyms.expansion: Candidate Key - Minimal super key that uniquely identifies tuples
acronyms.usage: Potential Primary Key; must be unique and not contain redundant attributes

acronyms.expansion: Alternate Key - Candidate key not chosen as Primary Key
acronyms.usage: Acts as secondary unique identifier; can be used for lookups

acronyms.expansion: Super Key - Set of attributes that uniquely identify tuples
acronyms.usage: Parent of all keys; may contain redundant attributes

visualTips.visualization: Draw a table with labeled columns (attributes) and numbered rows (tuples). Mark the header row as the schema. Show arrows pointing to different parts: 'Columns = Attributes', 'Rows = Tuples', 'Header = Relation Name'.
Create a pyramid: Bottom = Super Key (largest, includes all keys), Middle Layer = Candidate Keys (minimal), Top = Primary Key (one selected). Show Alternate Keys as side branches from Candidate Keys.
Draw two tables side by side. In Table A, highlight FK column with dashed border. In Table B, show matching PK column. Draw bidirectional arrow between them labeled 'REFERENCES'.
Show a table where single columns have duplicates, but combination of two columns together is unique. Use color to highlight the two-column combination that makes each row unique.

analogies.analogy: Like Aadhaar/SSN number - uniquely identifies one person among billions, never changes, and no two people share the same number.
Like a library book card having a member ID - the book 'references' the member table, linking who borrowed what.
Like multiple unique identifiers: Roll Number, Email, Aadhaar - any one could be the main (Primary) ID, but you choose one.
Like having both 'Full Name + Date of Birth' to identify someone - it works but has extra info you don't need (redundant).
All relations are tables but not all tables are relations. A relation is a mathematically defined table with no duplicate rows, ordered columns, or nested values - like a 'perfect' table.
NULL is like leaving a blank on a form when you don't know the answer yet - it's not '0' (we know there's nothing) nor 'empty space' - it's genuinely unknown.
